one_line_takeaway: Empirically testing 7 MCP clients against 4 hand-built tool-poisoning attacks with the model held constant (mostly Claude Sonnet 4.5), the authors find security outcome is driven by client-side architecture, not model choice -- Cursor failed all 4 attacks (no static validation, no warnings) while Claude Desktop and Cline blocked most of them, and 5 of 7 clients implement no static validation of server-provided tool metadata at all.
key_finding: Attack success varied dramatically by client despite most using the same underlying model (Claude Sonnet 4.5): Cursor was unsafe on all 4/4 attack types (reading files, logging/surveillance, phishing links, remote code execution) with no static validation, no parameter inspection, and no security warnings. Claude Desktop was safe on 3/4 (partial on the logging attack) via model-level refusals. Cline was safe on 3/4 but unsafe on remote code execution. Per the security-feature comparison (Table 24), 5 of 7 clients implement no static validation of tool metadata and 2 implement only partial validation; none of the 7 implement full ('Yes'-level) systematic static validation. Parameter visibility was rated 'High' only for Cline; two clients (Cursor, Langflow) were rated 'Low'. The paper concludes client architecture/trust-model choices, not model choice, are the dominant determinant of security outcome (same model, Claude Sonnet 4.5, produced both fully safe and fully unsafe results depending on the client).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (as distinct from the extensive Related Work in Section 2) is twofold: (1) a STRIDE + DREAD threat-modeling exercise across five MCP architectural components (MCP Host+Client, LLM, MCP Server, External Data Stores, Authorization Server) that catalogs 57 threats and scores each with DREAD (0-50 scale), and (2) an original empirical evaluation of 7 production MCP clients (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, Continue, Gemini CLI, Claude Code, Langflow) against 4 hand-crafted tool-poisoning attacks the authors designed and ran themselves in November 2025. The authors explicitly distinguish their contribution from prior work: e.g. they note Hasan et al. [14] studied 1,899 MCP servers server-side, Wang et al. [41] built MCPTox testing LLM agents against 45 servers/353 tools (attack success up to 72.8% on o1-mini), and Wang et al. [43] tested one client (Cline) with multiple models -- whereas this paper is the first (per its own Research Gap claim in Sec 2.6) to hold the model constant (Claude Sonnet 4.5, except Gemini CLI which used Gemini 2.5 Pro) and vary the client, to isolate client-side defensive behavior specifically.
threat_model: Adversary controls or compromises an MCP server and embeds malicious natural-language instructions inside tool metadata (name/description/inputSchema) returned via the tools/list call. The victim user connects a legitimate MCP client to this malicious/compromised server and, due to 'approval fatigue,' tends to approve tool invocations without careful inspection. The client passes the unvalidated tool metadata into the LLM's context window; the LLM treats the description as an instruction (indirect prompt injection / 'tool poisoning'), and can be manipulated into exfiltrating data, invoking tools with hidden malicious parameters, or executing unintended actions -- without the user being aware. The paper explicitly scopes out server-side vulnerabilities, LLM training security, and transport-layer configuration as out of scope for its empirical portion (these are attributed to cited prior work, e.g. [14]).
limitations: Authors' own stated threats to validity: (1) DREAD severity scores in Tables 7-13 were assigned by the authors themselves and are acknowledged as subjective/author-biased, only partially anchored to an external reference (the Adversa AI 'Top 25 MCP Vulnerabilities' severity framework); (2) external validity is limited -- only 7 clients and 4 attack types were tested, and results reflect only the specific client/model versions evaluated in November 2025 (e.g. Claude Desktop 0.14.4, Cursor 1.6.45), which may not generalize to other configurations or later versions; (3) the controlled local test environment may not reflect production deployment conditions; (4) Execution Sandboxing was NOT empirically tested due to time/resource constraints -- ratings ('Yes'/'Possible'/'No'/'Unknown') are based on documentation and architectural description review only, explicitly flagged by the authors as future work.

datasets_used: No external dataset; original test corpus consisting of 4 authors-authored malicious MCP tool definitions (reading sensitive files, logging tool usage, phishing link creation, remote script execution), published on the authors' GitHub repository (nyit-vancouver/mcp-security)
quantitative_results: 57 total threats identified across 5 MCP components via STRIDE
DREAD score for LLM01 Prompt Injection: 50/50 (Critical) -- maximum possible score
DREAD score for Tool Poisoning (data-store threat #48): 46.5/50 (Critical)
DREAD score for Command Injection (server threat #32): 47.5/50 (Critical)
DREAD score for Remote Code Execution (server threat #33): 45/50 (Critical)

Attack success matrix (Table 19): Cursor unsafe on 4/4 attack types (reading files, logging, phishing, remote exec); Claude Desktop safe on 3/4 (partial on logging); Cline safe on 3/4 (unsafe on remote exec); Continue safe on 3/4 (partial on phishing); Gemini CLI safe on 3/4 (partial on reading files); Claude Code safe on 3/4 (partial on reading files); Langflow: partial on all 4
Security feature comparison (Table 24): 5 of 7 clients implement no static validation, 2 implement partial validation, 0 implement full/systematic validation
Parameter Visibility ratings: High for Cline only; Partial for Claude Desktop, Continue, Gemini CLI, Claude Code; Low for Cursor and Langflow
Injection Detection mechanism: Model-based (Claude Desktop), Pattern-based (Cline), Partial (Gemini CLI), None (Cursor, Continue, Claude Code, Langflow)
baselines_compared: No ML/algorithmic baselines -- this is a security evaluation study comparing 7 production MCP client implementations against each other: Claude Desktop for Windows 0.14.4, Cursor 1.6.45, Cline (VS Code extension) 3.34.0, Continue (VS Code extension) 1.2.10, Gemini CLI 0.9.0, Claude Code 2.0.25, Langflow 1.7

Cited (not reproduced) external results used for contextualization only: MCPTox [41] reporting attack success rates up to 72.8% on o1-mini and <3% refusal rate on Claude-3.7-Sonnet across 45 servers/353 tools/1,312 test cases; Hasan et al. [14] finding 7.2% of 1,899 open-source MCP servers had general vulnerabilities and 5.5% had MCP-specific tool-poisoning vectors
